message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "long-standing customer's second contract: cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7) → premium 160 G"
Expected behavior: premium for {yearsWithMHPCO:3}, [cursed sword ench 7], followUp:true = 160.

The full additive formula (per spec R4/R5/R8): start from policy base premium P = sum of item base premiums BEFORE item surcharges (here sword = 100).
- item-specific surcharges (on the affected item's base): cursed +50% of that item's base, high-enchantment (>=5) +30% of that item's base
- policy-wide modifiers (each computed as a percentage of the policy base P, added/subtracted additively, NOT compounded): loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO>=2) -20% of P; first insurance +10% of P (always applies — every quote item is treated as a first insurance); follow-up contract (when followUp option true) -15% of P
- then + 5 processing fee
- round in MHPCO favor: premium rounds UP (Math.ceil), intermediates kept as fractions

Check against the active premium tests:
- test 16 loyalty: {2 yrs}, sword, followUp:false → 100 -20 +10 +5 = 95 ✓
- test 18 newcomer: {0 yrs}, cursed sword ench3, followUp:false → 100 +50 curse +10 first-ins +5 = 165 ✓
- test 19: {3 yrs}, cursed sword ench7, followUp:true → 100 +50 curse +30 high-ench -20 loyalty +10 first-ins -15 follow-up +5 = 160 ✓
- empty list → 5 ✓

Replace the hardcoded premium ternary with this real computation. Keep ALL 18 prior tests green (including the basePremium-only assertions which must be unaffected).
Current error: expected 95 to be 160
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
I have the full context. The current premium is a hardcoded ternary (empty→5, loyal→95, newcomer→165). The failing test 19 expects 160. 

This is cycle 19. There are now multiple premium tests active (16: 95, 18: 165, 19: 160, empty: 5). A hardcoded `if` per case would be growing comically long and fragile, and the prompt explicitly instructs me to replace the hardcoded ternary with the real additive computation. The general formula is now forced — three distinct premium values across three distinct modifier combinations cannot be faked cleanly without effectively encoding the formula anyway.

Let me verify the formula against all active premium tests:

- **empty**: 5 (fee only) ✓
- **test 16** loyalty {2yr}, plain sword, followUp:false: P=100; loyalty -20, first-ins +10, fee +5 → 100-20+10+5 = 95 ✓
- **test 18** newcomer {0yr}, cursed sword ench3, followUp:false: P=100; curse +50, first-ins +10, fee +5 → 100+50+10+5 = 165 ✓
- **test 19** {3yr}, cursed sword ench7, followUp:true: P=100; curse +50, high-ench +30, loyalty -20, first-ins +10, follow-up -15, fee +5 → 160 ✓

The policy base P = `baseWithoutSurcharges`. Item surcharges are already computed in `surcharges`. Now I implement the real premium computation.

content.text:
Got 130, expected 160. The cursed sword ench7 has both cursed AND enchantment>=5, but `surchargeFactorFor` only applies ONE (cursed wins via early return). So `basePremium` = 100 + 50 (curse only) = 150, missing the +30 high-ench.

Let me verify: 150 base − 20 loyalty (20% of 100) + 10 first-ins − 15 follow-up + 5 = 150-20+10-15+5 = 130. The spec says both surcharges should stack: +50 curse AND +30 high-ench.

But wait — test 13 (cursed sword alone → 150) and test 14 (ench5 sword → 130) each test one surcharge in isolation. Test 19 is the first to combine cursed+high-ench on the same item, expecting both to apply additively (+50 +30). The current `surchargeFactorFor` uses `if/else` early returns — a fake from cycles 13/14 when only one surcharge was ever present. Test 19 now breaks that fake, so I generalize the surcharge to be additive.

content.text:
Green Phase Complete.

**Implementation**: Replaced the hardcoded premium ternary in `quote` with the real additive formula. Policy-wide modifiers are each computed as a percentage of the policy base P (= `baseWithoutSurcharges`) and added/subtracted additively: loyalty (yearsWithMHPCO>=2) −20%, first-insurance (non-empty list) +10%, follow-up (option) −15%; then `Math.ceil(premium + 5)` for the processing fee with round-up in MHPCO favor. I also had to generalize `surchargeFactorFor`'s `if/else` early-return (a fake from cycles 13/14, where only one surcharge was ever present at a time) into an additive `surchargeRateFor` so a cursed AND high-enchantment item stacks +50% +30% on the same item base.

**Result**: All tests now pass (19 passing, 21 todo).

**Approach**: This was the cycle where the formula could no longer be faked. Three distinct premium values (95, 165, 160) across three distinct modifier combinations plus the empty-list case (5) cannot be branched-on cleanly without effectively encoding the formula. The surcharge generalization was forced specifically by test 19: it is the first test combining `cursed` + `enchantment>=5` on one item, which broke the prior single-surcharge fake — exactly the "generalize only when a test breaks the fake" trigger. Tests 13 and 14 still verify each surcharge in isolation and remain green; all 12 basePremium-only assertions are unaffected since `basePremium` continues to be returned unchanged.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-22_10-51-43_claim-office-example-mapping_v4.2.1-fake-it-green_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
